name: Aburayama Forest Park
access: Bus from Hakata or Tenjin (~30 min)
best_season: Year-round; spring (azaleas) and autumn (foliage) are most scenic
description: Fukuoka's green lung — a forested mountain park just 30 minutes from the city center with mountain biking trails, a kids' forest museum, scenic viewpoints, and glamping facilities. On weekends it attracts Fukuoka's athletic community for trail running and mountain biking; on weekdays it's blissfully quiet.

name: Akizuki Castle Ruins
address: 朝倉市秋月, Asakura City, Fukuoka
access: Bus from Amagase station (JR Kyudai Line) ~20 min; 90 min total from Fukuoka
description: A serene castle-town deep in the mountains 60km from Fukuoka, preserved by accident of geography — the Kuroda clan seat that never had to modernize. The approach road lined with 200-year-old cherry trees is among Kyushu's most beautiful.

name: Dazaifu Tenmangu
access: 5 min walk from Nishitetsu Dazaifu Station
description: One of Japan's most important Shinto shrines, dedicated to Sugawara no Michizane, the deity of learning. Surrounded by 6,000 plum trees that burst into bloom each February.

name: Fukuoka Castle Ruins & Maizuru Park
access: 15 min walk from Akasaka subway station or Tenjin
best_season: Late March–early April (cherry blossoms) or autumn
description: The remains of one of Kyushu's largest castles, built in 1607 for the powerful Kuroda clan. The stone walls, moats, and surviving turrets still impress despite the main tower's disappearance. In spring, 1,300 cherry trees turn the castle grounds into one of Fukuoka's best hanami (cherry blossom viewing) spots — and it's completely free.

name: Fukuoka Yatai Stalls
address: Nakagawa, Chuo-ku, Fukuoka (riverside between Tenjin and Nakasu)
access: Tenjin or Nakasu-Kawabata subway stations
description: Fukuoka's iconic open-air food stalls that pack out along the Naka River and Tenjin every evening. Squeeze in for tonkotsu ramen, yakitori, and cold beer under the city lights.

name: Hakata Machiya Folk Museum
access: 5 min walk from Gion subway station
description: Three authentic Meiji and Taisho-era Hakata townhouses relocated and meticulously preserved to show how merchant life looked in old Hakata. The museum also houses the city's finest collection of Gion Yamakasa festival artifacts — the giant festival floats (kazariyama) that are displayed year-round.

name: Itoshima Peninsula
access: JR Chikuhi Line from Hakata to Chikuzen-Maebaru (~30 min), then taxi/bike
description: A laid-back coastal peninsula just 30 minutes from Fukuoka city with sweeping ocean views, oyster shacks, sunset Instagram spots, and a growing craft scene.

name: Keya no Oto Sea Cave
access: Showa Bus from Maebaru Station (~30 min) or drive
best_season: Year-round; most dramatic in winter storms (stay safely on the path)
description: The largest sea cave in Japan, carved 64 metres high and 10 metres wide into the volcanic basalt coast of the Itoshima Peninsula. At high tide, waves boom against the walls with a sound like thunder (oto means sound). A 1-km clifftop trail leads to the cave entrance where you can stand at the lip and look straight down into crashing surf.

name: Kushida Shrine
address: 博多区上川端町, Hakata Ward, Fukuoka
access: 5 min walk from Nakasu-Kawabata subway station
description: Hakata's guardian shrine and birthplace of the Gion Yamakasa festival — every July, enormous float-mountains weighing over 1 tonne are raced through city streets at 4am. Outside festival season, a peaceful enclave in downtown Hakata.

name: Mojiko Retro District
access: JR Kagoshima Main Line to Moji Station (~1 hr from Hakata)
description: A beautifully preserved Meiji-era port town at the northern tip of Kyushu. European-style brick buildings, a pedestrian waterfront, and Japan's best grilled curry.

name: Munakata Taisha
address: 宗像市田島, Munakata City, Fukuoka
access: Nishitetsu Bus from Tenjin ~45 min; JR Munakata-Tamashima station
description: A UNESCO World Heritage shrine complex of three separate shrines dedicated to three goddesses of the sea. The outermost island, Okinoshima, is so sacred no women have ever set foot on it and visiting men must ritually bathe before landing.

name: Nanzoin Temple
access: 2 min walk from JR Kido-Nanzoin Mae Station (Sasaguri Line from Hakata, ~30 min)
description: Home to the world's largest bronze reclining Buddha statue at 41 metres long — bigger than the Statue of Liberty. Set deep in cedar forest, this temple has a deeply serene atmosphere.

name: Shofukuji Temple
access: 10 min walk from Hakata Station
description: Japan's first Zen temple, founded in 1195 by Eisai who brought tea seeds back from China — making this the birthplace of Japanese tea culture. A hidden gem in the heart of Hakata.

name: Uminonakamichi Seaside Park
access: JR Kagoshima Line to Uminonakamichi Station (from Hakata ~30 min)
description: A national seaside park on the narrow peninsula enclosing Hakata Bay. 350 hectares of flower gardens, cycling paths, animal park, and amusement zone — different flower species bloom each month, from tulips and poppies in spring to cosmos and dahlias in autumn. The cycling paths run along the bay with views back toward Fukuoka city.

name: Yanagibashi Rengo Market
access: Watanabe-dori subway station, 5 min walk
description: Hakata's "kitchen" — a covered market of 35 specialist shops that has supplied the city's restaurants and home cooks since 1910. Fishmongers, vegetable sellers, tofu makers, pickle shops, and ready-to-eat food stalls pack the narrow aisles. The morning energy is extraordinary: chefs negotiating with fishmongers over the overnight Genkai Sea catch.

name: Amami Oshima
access: Flight from Kagoshima airport (~45 min); ferry from Kagoshima port (~11 hrs)
admission: Island free; mangrove kayak rental from ¥2,500
description: A subtropical island midway between Kyushu and Okinawa with UNESCO World Heritage rainforest, mangrove kayaking, and some of the clearest sea in Japan. Amami has its own distinct culture — different music, food, and weaving tradition (Oshima Tsumugi silk) — and far fewer foreign visitors than Okinawa.

name: Chiran Peace Museum for Kamikaze Pilots
access: Bus from Kagoshima-Chuo Station to Chiran (~1 hr), then 5 min walk
description: The most humanizing war museum in Japan. Chiran was a major kamikaze base — 1,036 young pilots departed from here for their final missions in 1945. The museum preserves their handwritten farewell letters (many expressing love for family, not ideology), personal photographs, and the Zero fighters they flew. Profoundly moving regardless of background.

name: Chiran Samurai Residence District
access: Bus from Kagoshima-Chuo Station (~1 hr) or rental car
description: Seven perfectly preserved samurai residences with stone-walled gardens in the "Little Kyoto of Satsuma." Each garden is a distinct masterwork of Japanese landscape design — rock arrangements, clipped hedges, and borrowed scenery using Chiran's forested mountains. The moss-covered stone walls along the approach lane are among the most photogenic in all of Japan.

name: Ibusuki Sand Baths
access: 特急 Ibusuki-no-Tamatebako from Kagoshima-Chuo (~50 min), then 10 min walk
description: The world's only natural geothermal sand bath. Attendants bury you in volcanic black sand heated by underground hot springs to 50°C as the ocean laps a few metres away.

name: Iso Nagisa Foot Onsen
access: City bus #1 from Kagoshima-Chuo Station (~25 min)
description: A free outdoor foot bath set directly in volcanic lava rock on the shore of Kagoshima Bay, just steps from Sengan-en Garden. The naturally hot spring water flows through channels carved into the ancient lava, and you sit with your feet in the water looking directly at Sakurajima across the bay — a view and experience you will not find anywhere else.

name: Kaimondake Volcano
access: JR Ibusuki-Makurazaki Line to Kaimon Station (from Kagoshima-Chuo ~1 hr), then 30 min walk to trailhead
description: A near-perfect conical stratovolcano nicknamed "Satsuma Fuji," rising 924m from the Satsuma Peninsula coast. The 3-hour circular hiking trail offers panoramic views of the Satsuma Peninsula, distant Yakushima and Kuchierabu islands, and Ibusuki's sand bath coast — one of the finest volcano summits in Kyushu.

name: Kirishima Jingu Shrine
access: Bus from JR Kirishima-Jingu Station (~20 min)
description: One of Kyushu's oldest and most powerful shrines, dedicated to Ninigi-no-Mikoto who descended from heaven to begin the divine lineage of Japanese emperors. Set at 600m altitude in misty volcanic highlands.

name: Sakurajima
access: 15 min ferry from Kagoshima port (¥200, runs every 15 min)
best_season: Year-round (best views on clear winter days)
description: Japan's most active volcano — a brooding island connected to the mainland by a 1914 lava flow. It erupts hundreds of times a year, dusting Kagoshima city with ash and producing the world's largest radishes.

name: Sengan-en Garden & Shoko Shuseikan
access: City bus #1 from Kagoshima-Chuo Station (~25 min)
best_season: Year-round, spring plum blossoms are spectacular
description: A stunning 17th-century samurai garden set against Sakurajima volcano with Kagoshima Bay as its "borrowed scenery." Adjacent to Japan's first Western-style industrial factory complex (now a museum).

name: Shiroyama Observatory
access: City bus #1 from Kagoshima-Chuo Station (~20 min) or 40 min walk
best_season: Year-round; best at sunrise or sunset
description: A forested hilltop rising 107m behind Kagoshima city center — the site of the last stand of the Satsuma Rebellion in 1877, where Saigo Takamori died. Today the observation deck gives the city's definitive view: the entire bay, the active Sakurajima volcano perfectly framed, and on clear days, the Kirishima mountain range beyond.

name: Tenmonkan Food District
access: 10 min walk from Kagoshima-Chuo Station or tram to Tenmonkan-dori stop
description: Kagoshima's central covered shopping arcade and the heart of its food culture. This is where to find all of the prefecture's signature dishes: shirokuma (polar bear) shaved ice, Berkshire black pork (kurobuta) shabu-shabu, chicken-sashimi (tori no tataki), and shochu distilleries offering samples. The covered arcade stays active until midnight.

name: Yakushima Island
access: Ferry from Kagoshima Takachiho Port: high-speed jet foil ~2 hrs (¥8,900) or overnight car ferry (~10 hrs)
admission: Island free; Jomon Sugi trail ¥1,000 trail maintenance fee
description: A UNESCO World Heritage island of ancient cedar forests where trees live for thousands of years. The iconic Jomon Sugi cedar — estimated at 2,170 to 7,200 years old — is the oldest living thing in Japan. About 35% of the island is national park, with more rainfall than almost anywhere else in Japan feeding waterfalls that cascade through mossy old-growth.

name: Amakusa Sakitsu Village
address: Sakitsu, Kawaura Town, Amakusa City, Kumamoto
access: 2.5 hrs by bus from Kumamoto Station via Amakusa
best_season: Year-round; best photographed at low tide for church reflections
description: A UNESCO World Heritage fishing village where a Catholic church sits at the water's edge — a legacy of hidden Christians who secretly practiced their faith for 250 years during Japan's Edo period ban on Christianity.

name: Daikanbo Overlook
access: 30 min by car from Aso Station via Milk Road scenic drive
best_season: Autumn (October–November) for best cloud seas; Spring for green caldera
description: The highest viewpoint on Aso's outer caldera rim, offering a full 360-degree panorama of the world's largest caldera and all five Aso peaks. This is the one spot where the true scale of the landscape becomes overwhelming.

name: Hitoyoshi Castle Ruins
access: 2 hrs from Kumamoto by JR Hisatsu line
description: Perched above the Kuma River, these haunting castle ruins offer an authentic slice of feudal Japan with almost no crowds. Boat rafting on the river below.

name: Kamishikimi Kumanoimasu Shrine
access: 20 min by car from Minami-Aso Station
best_season: Spring (fresh green) and Autumn (cedar forest mist)
description: A haunting mountain shrine reached through 93 stone lanterns lining a moss-covered stairway into cedar forest. The giant split boulder at the summit — Ugeto-iwa — is said to have been pierced by a deity's spear, and stepping through it is believed to grant wishes.

name: Kikuchi Gorge
access: 90 min by bus from Kumamoto; 30 min by car from Kikuchi IC
description: An ancient beech forest gorge with water so clear it's designated one of Japan's 100 Best Waters. The blue-green stream cuts through primeval hardwood forest barely touched by tourism — swimming holes in summer, flaming foliage in autumn.

name: Kumamoto Castle
access: 15 min walk from Kumamoto Station; 5 min by tram
description: One of Japan's three great castles, famed for its "impossible" curved stone walls. Badly damaged in the 2016 earthquake, its ongoing restoration has become a symbol of the city's resilience — the repair work itself is now part of the attraction.

name: Kurokawa Onsen Village
access: 2 hours from Kumamoto Station by car/bus
description: A hidden hot spring village tucked in a river valley. No neon signs — just rustic ryokan with private outdoor baths. Japan's most romantic onsen town.

name: Kusasenri-ga-hama Meadow
access: 20 min by bus from Aso Station; 10 min by car from Nakadake Crater
best_season: Year-round; Spring for vivid green, Autumn for golden grass
description: A vast circular meadow at 1,100m elevation on Mt. Aso, where horses graze around a mirror-flat pond while Nakadake volcano smokes in the background. One of the most surreal open landscapes in Japan.

name: Mt. Aso Caldera
access: 90 min by bus from Kumamoto Station
description: One of the world's largest active calderas. Hike the lunar-like rim of Nakadake crater and gaze into a turquoise sulfuric lake few tourists ever see up close.

name: Nabegataki Falls
access: 20 min by car from Kurokawa Onsen; advance reservation required
best_season: Spring (May) and Autumn; reservations required on weekends
description: A wide lacy curtain of water falls across porous volcanic rock formed by Aso's eruptions 90,000 years ago — and you can walk behind it. The cave ledge behind the falls is cool even in midsummer and utterly quiet.

name: Reigando Cave (Musashi's Cave)
access: 40 min by bus from Kumamoto Station
best_season: Any season — cool and atmospheric year-round
description: The very cave where legendary swordsman Miyamoto Musashi wrote 'The Book of Five Rings'. A meditative walk through cedar forest leads to this sacred mountain grotto.

name: Suizenji Jojuen Garden
access: 15 min by tram from Kumamoto Station
description: A 17th-century strolling garden shaped to miniaturize the 53 post towns of the Tokaido highway. The tiny Mt. Fuji replica in the center is oddly satisfying.

name: Tsuujun Bridge (National Treasure Aqueduct)
access: 90 min by car from Kumamoto City; limited bus service
description: Japan's only stone arch aqueduct, hand-built in 1854 by villagers to bring water across a valley to drought-stricken farmland. During scheduled releases, water jets horizontally from the bridge's sides in a spectacle unchanged for 170 years.

name: Ama-no-Iwato Shrine & Amanoyasukawara
access: 15 min by car from Takachiho town center
best_season: Year-round; autumn morning mist adds mystical atmosphere
description: The sacred cave where sun goddess Amaterasu hid, plunging the world into darkness — and the vast riverbed cavern where 8 million deities gathered to coax her back out. Today the cavern is lined with thousands of stone cairns stacked by visitors, creating an atmosphere unlike anywhere else in Japan.

name: Aoshima Island & Devil's Washboard
access: 30 min by train from Miyazaki Station
best_season: Subtropical — warm year-round; avoid typhoon season (Aug-Sep)
description: A tiny jungle-covered island connected to the mainland by a 300m bridge, surrounded by 'Devil's Washboard' — perfectly rippled rock formations shaped by millennia of ocean waves.

name: Aya Teruhane Suspension Bridge
access: 40 min by car from Miyazaki City
best_season: Autumn (late October–November) for spectacular canopy color
description: A 250m suspension bridge spanning a ravine above one of Asia's last intact broadleaf evergreen forests — ancient trees up to 800 years old in a UNESCO Biosphere Reserve. In autumn the canopy below turns gold and crimson while you walk across.

name: Cape Toi Wild Horses
access: 2 hrs by car from Miyazaki Station (no public transport)
description: Japan's only herd of truly wild horses roams the dramatic cliffs and grasslands of this southern cape. Walk among them at sunrise when morning mist rolls in from Osumi Strait.

name: Ebino Plateau
access: 90 min by car from Miyazaki Station; 30 min from Kobayashi IC
best_season: Late May–June (azaleas); Autumn for clear skies and fall colors
description: A volcanic highland at 1,200m in the Kirishima range with a chain of emerald and cobalt crater lakes, and Japan's most spectacular azalea blooms in late spring. The lake circuit trail passes three different-colored volcanic lakes within a 2-hour walk.

name: Obi Castle Town
access: 10 min by bus from Obi Station (JR Nichinan Line)
best_season: Spring (cherry blossoms on the castle grounds) and Autumn
description: The most intact feudal castle town in Kyushu — locals call it the "Little Kyoto of Kyushu." Samurai estates, merchant quarters, and a pine-lined castle approach are preserved in a town that has barely changed since the Edo period.

name: Sun Messe Nichinan (Moai Statues)
access: 60 min by bus from Miyazaki Station; 30 min by car
best_season: Year-round; Spring for blue skies and clear Pacific views
description: The only place outside Easter Island with official full-size moai replica statues — seven figures overlooking the Pacific, authorized by the Rapa Nui Council. Each moai represents a different area of good fortune and can be touched for blessings.

name: Takachiho Gorge
access: 90 min by express bus from Kumamoto
description: Rent a rowboat and drift through a canyon carved by the Gokase River, under towering basalt walls and a waterfall that drops directly into the gorge. Go at 8am before the crowds arrive.

name: Takachiho Shrine Night Kagura
access: Inside Takachiho Shrine
description: Watch masked priests perform ancient Shinto kagura dances telling the myth of Amaterasu emerging from a cave — every night at 8pm, year-round. One of Japan's most atmospheric cultural rituals.

name: Udo Jingu Shrine (Sea Cave)
access: 90 min by bus from Miyazaki Station
description: A Shinto shrine built entirely inside a sea cave on a cliff above crashing Pacific waves. Access via steep stone steps carved into the cliff face. Buy mudball offerings and throw them into a rock.

name: Umagase Cliffs, Hyuga Cape
access: 15 min by car from Hyuga Station (JR Nippo Line)
best_season: Clear weather year-round; dramatically stormy in winter
description: Sheer 70m basalt columns plunge straight into the Pacific along the most dramatic stretch of Japan's eastern coastline. A glass-floor observation deck lets you stand over the void above crashing waves — and the horizon here is uninterrupted ocean all the way to the Americas.

name: Umisachi Yamasachi Sightseeing Train
access: Departs Miyazaki Station (reservation required)
best_season: Spring and Autumn for clear skies
description: A retro sightseeing train that hugs the Pacific coastline south from Miyazaki City. The ocean is so close you can see waves breaking meters away. Local obento included on some services.

name: Dejima Dutch Trading Post
access: 10 min walk from Nagasaki Station or tram to Dejima stop
description: For 200 years during Japan's isolation period, this tiny fan-shaped artificial island was the only point of contact between Japan and the Western world. Dutch traders lived here under strict conditions; their books, clocks, and scientific instruments slowly changed Japan. The island has been meticulously reconstructed to its 1820s appearance, complete with furnished warehouses, a VOC flag, and Dutch gardens.

name: Glover Garden
access: 15 min walk from Oura Tenshudo-Shita tram stop
description: An open-air hilltop museum of Western-style residences built by Meiji-era foreign merchants. Thomas Glover's stone villa (1863) is Japan's oldest surviving Western-style house, with sweeping harbor views.

name: Goto Islands
access: Ferry from Nagasaki port (~3 hrs to Fukue Island) or flight (~30 min)
description: An archipelago of 140 islands where 30,000 "hidden Christians" (kakure kirishitan) secretly maintained their faith for 250 years under the death penalty. The UNESCO-listed churches, set in fishing villages against a backdrop of spectacularly clear emerald sea, represent one of the world's most extraordinary stories of religious perseverance.

name: Hashima (Gunkanjima)
access: Guided boat tour from Nagasaki port (~40 min each way)
best_season: April–October (tours run when seas are calm)
description: The haunting abandoned island known as "Battleship Island" — a former undersea coal mine that once housed 5,000 people in the world's highest population density. Now a UNESCO World Heritage site.

name: Huis Ten Bosch
access: JR Huis Ten Bosch Station from Hakata (~2 hrs direct) or Nagasaki (~1.5 hrs)
description: A meticulously built 152-hectare recreation of a Dutch town on the shores of Omura Bay — canals, windmills, gabled buildings, and tulip fields transplanted to Kyushu. Japan's most elaborate theme park runs some of the country's most ambitious light shows, including a 13-million-bulb winter illumination that has won the Guinness World Record for largest.

name: Meganebashi (Spectacles Bridge)
access: 10 min walk from Nagasaki Station
best_season: Year-round; spring for cherry blossoms over the river
description: Japan's oldest stone arch bridge, built in 1634 by a Chinese Zen monk. When the Nakashima River is calm, the twin arches reflect perfectly in the water below, creating the "spectacles" (megane) shape that gave the bridge its name. The bridge survived the 1945 atomic bombing intact and remains the heart of Nagasaki's historic riverside quarter.

name: Mount Inasa Night View Observatory
access: Tram to Fuchi Station, then 10 min walk to ropeway base
description: One of Japan's three great night views — Nagasaki's harbor, peninsula, and islands create a "million-dollar night view" when seen from 333m. The city's geography (built on hills around a narrow bay) produces a more complex, layered nightscape than flat cities like Kobe or Hakodate, making this arguably the finest of the three.

name: Nagasaki Chinatown (Shinchi)
access: 5 min walk from Nagasaki Station or tram to Tsuki-machi
description: Japan's oldest and smallest Chinatown, established in the 1680s. Just two crossing streets packed with chanpon restaurants (Nagasaki's signature noodle dish) and pork bun stalls.

name: Nagasaki Peace Park
access: Tram to Matsuyamamachi stop, 5 min walk
description: Built near the atomic bomb hypocenter, this park pairs the hopeful Peace Statue (a 10-meter bronze figure) with the sobering Atomic Bomb Museum below. One of the most moving sites in Japan.

name: Nagasaki Shinchi & Lantern Festival Area
access: 5 min walk from Nagasaki Station or tram to Tsuki-machi
best_season: Year-round; late January–February for the Lantern Festival
description: Japan's oldest and most atmospheric Chinatown, where the annual Lantern Festival (Nagasaki Lantern Festival) transforms the city every Chinese New Year with 15,000 lanterns hanging over the streets. The event is Nagasaki's biggest celebration — lion dances, Chinese opera, and emperor processions fill the harbor area for 15 days.

name: Oura Cathedral
access: Tram to Oura Tenshudo-Shita stop, 5 min walk
description: Japan's only National Treasure cathedral — a Gothic limestone church consecrated in 1865, built to memorialize the 26 Christians martyred in Nagasaki in 1597. Shortly after opening, the discovery of thousands of "hidden Christians" who had secretly practiced their faith for 250 years astonished the world and was called the "miracle of Oura." Now a UNESCO World Heritage site.

name: Unzen Onsen & Hell Springs
access: Bus from Nagasaki or Shimabara (~1.5 hrs)
admission: Free (hell springs boardwalk); onsen bath from ¥500
description: A volcano-top hot spring resort surrounded by 30 active sulfur vents known as Unzen Jigoku (Hells). The town sits inside Unzen-Amakusa National Park with dramatic hiking trails above the clouds.

name: Beppu Jigoku (Hell) Tour
access: 5 min by bus from Beppu Station
best_season: Year-round; misty winter is most atmospheric
description: Eight 'hells' of boiling springs in otherworldly colors: blood red, cobalt blue, milk white. The Umi Jigoku (Sea Hell) is strangely beautiful — and you can eat eggs boiled in its steam.

name: Harajiri Falls
access: 90 min by car from Oita Station
best_season: Rainy season (June–July) for max water; Spring and Autumn for clear air
description: Japan's widest waterfall — a 120m curtain of water dropping 20m over hexagonal basalt columns shaped by ancient Aso volcanic flows. Called "East Asia's Niagara," it's most spectacular after heavy rain when the entire rock face disappears under white water.

name: Kokonoe "Yume" Grand Suspension Bridge
access: 60 min by car from Yufu IC (Oita Expressway)
description: Japan's highest pedestrian suspension bridge hangs 173m above a forested river gorge. Walk the 390m span as the canyon drops away beneath you. In autumn, the gorge becomes a blazing sea of red and orange maples — visible from both ends of the bridge.

name: Kuju Highlands (Hana Mitate)
access: 90 min by car from Oita Station
best_season: Spring (April–May) for azaleas; Autumn (Oct) for susuki
description: Japan's largest highland wildflower meadow. In late summer, millions of susuki silver grass plumes sway across volcanic hills. In spring, millions of wild azaleas paint the slopes pink.

name: Kunisaki Peninsula Buddhist Trail
access: 45 min by car from Oita Airport
best_season: Spring and Autumn; February for the fire ritual
description: A circular peninsula where a unique fusion of Buddhism and Shinto flourished 1,300 years ago. Ancient cliff temples, stone-carved kannon statues on forest paths, and fire-walking rituals that survive unchanged to this day — and almost no foreign visitors.

name: Oka Castle Ruins
access: 90 min by car from Oita Station; 5 min from Bungo-Taketa Station
description: A 12th-century mountain fortress perched on sheer cliffs above a river gorge — the place where composer Rentaro Taki played as a child and found inspiration for "Kojo no Tsuki," one of Japan's best-loved songs. Almost no foreign tourists ever come here.

name: Takasakiyama Monkey Mountain
access: 10 min by bus from Oita Station
description: Over 1,500 wild Japanese macaques roam free on this mountain above Beppu Bay. You walk among them on a mountain path — they ignore you completely. Babies cling to their mothers all winter.

name: Usa Jingu Shrine
access: 30 min by JR Nippo Line from Oita Station
description: Japan's first and most important Hachiman shrine, founded in 725 AD. The original model for 40,000 Hachiman shrines across Japan. Visit on a weekday to have it almost to yourself.

name: Usuki Stone Buddhas
access: 30 min by car from Usuki Station (JR Nippo Line)
best_season: Spring and Autumn; early morning for soft light and solitude
description: Sixty Buddhist carvings emerge from a sheer mossy rock face in a cedar forest clearing — Japan's only National Treasure-designated stone carvings. Made over 1,000 years ago, the identity of their creators and their original purpose remain a mystery.

name: Yabakei Gorge
access: 30 min by car from Nakatsu IC; limited bus service
description: One of Japan's three great scenic landscapes — a 36km corridor of volcanic tuff pillars, bizarre pinnacles, and dense hardwood forest. Almost unknown outside Japan, yet the autumn foliage here rivals anything in Kyoto.

name: Yufuin Morning Mist Valley
access: 2.5 hrs from Fukuoka by direct Yufuin no Mori train
best_season: Autumn and Winter for the densest mist
description: Arrive before 9am to catch the valley floor wrapped in thermal mist rising from the earth. No photo filter needed. Then wander Yufuin's art galleries and cheese shops.

name: Yufuin Yunohira-kaido Street
access: 5 min walk from Yufuin Station
best_season: Year-round; Autumn for foliage while strolling
description: Yufuin's main pedestrian street, lined with farm-to-table cafes, local cheese shops, craft workshops, and a Cotswolds-inspired Floral Village. The best place in Kyushu to eat wagyu beef, drink pour-over coffee, and browse Japanese craft goods at your own pace.

name: Arita Pottery Town
access: JR Sasebo Line from Hakata to Arita (~1.5 hr)
description: The birthplace of Japanese porcelain, where ceramic production began in 1616 after kaolin clay was discovered. The main street is lined with kilns, galleries, and manufacturers exporting worldwide.

name: Higashiyoka Tidal Flat
access: Car recommended (40 min from Saga Station); limited bus service
description: A Ramsar Convention wetland on Ariake Sea — one of Japan's most biodiverse tidal flats, home to 140 species of migratory birds and the spectacle of mudskippers and fiddler crabs at low tide. In autumn, the shichimenso (salicornia) turns the mudflat a vivid red, creating an otherworldly landscape.

name: Imari Okawachi-yama Kiln Village
access: Bus from Imari Station (~20 min) or taxi
best_season: Year-round; early November for the kiln market
description: A hidden valley where the Nabeshima clan's secret kilns produced porcelain exclusively for the Edo shogunate and European royalty. Today 30+ kilns still operate in this quiet mountain valley — you can watch master potters at work and buy directly from the kilns that supplied Versailles.

name: Karatsu Castle
access: JR Chikuhi Line or Karatsu Line from Fukuoka Hakata to Karatsu (~1.5 hr), then 10 min walk
best_season: April (cherry blossoms around castle) or autumn
description: A beautifully restored castle perched on a pine-covered promontory over Karatsu Bay, nicknamed "Dancing Crane Castle." The view over the bay from the top floor is stunning.

name: Mifuneyama Rakuen Garden
access: 5 min taxi from Takeo-Onsen Station or free shuttle during peak season
description: A 500,000 sq metre garden carved into the cliffs of a 235-meter granite mountain, created by the lord of Takeo Domain in 1845. Famous for 200,000 azalea bushes in spring and fiery maple foliage in autumn — one of Kyushu's great seasonal spectacles.

name: Nanatsugama Sea Caves
access: Bus from Karatsu Station to Yobuko (~30 min), then 10 min walk to port
best_season: April–October (boat tours operate in calm weather)
description: Seven sea caves (nana = seven, kama = pot) carved into volcanic basalt cliffs on the Genkai coast. The largest cave reaches 110 metres deep with a 16-metre ceiling. Access by sightseeing boat lets you glide right inside the cave chambers as waves echo around you.

name: Niji-no-Matsubara Beach
access: 20 min walk from Karatsu Station along the coast
description: One of Japan's Three Great Pine Groves — a 5 km arc of white sand beach backed by half a million pine trees. The trees were planted 400 years ago to protect the town from sea winds, and today the combination of turquoise water, white sand, and dark pines is simply extraordinary.

name: Saga Castle & History Museum
access: 15 min walk from Saga Station
description: Saga Castle was the seat of the Nabeshima clan for 270 years, and its partial reconstruction now houses one of Japan's best-designed samurai history museums. The moat, stone walls, and the magnificent Tamon Yagura gate tower are original — some of the finest surviving examples of Edo-period castle architecture in Kyushu.

name: Saga International Balloon Fiesta Museum
access: 15 min walk from Saga Station
description: Saga hosts Asia's largest hot air balloon festival each autumn, attracting 100+ balloons from 20+ countries. The year-round museum lets you experience a balloon gondola simulator, see the engineering behind it, and understand why Saga's flat terrain and calm dawn winds make it the world's best balloon location.

name: Takeo Onsen
access: JR Nagasaki Line to Takeo-Onsen Station (from Hakata ~1 hr by limited express)
best_season: Year-round; autumn for foliage around the gate
description: One of Japan's oldest hot spring towns with a 1,300-year history, anchored by a magnificent vermilion gate (romon) that has become the symbol of Saga Prefecture. The spring water is a smooth, skin-softening alkaline type beloved by locals for centuries.

name: Yoshinogari Historical Park
access: 3 min walk from JR Yoshinogari-Koen Station (Nagasaki Line from Hakata ~35 min)
admission: ¥460 adults, free Sat & Sun for basic areas
description: Japan's largest and most complete Yayoi period (300 BC – 300 AD) village reconstruction. Walk through ancient moat-encircled settlements and watchtowers on a 98-hectare site.

name: Yutoku Inari Shrine
access: Bus from JR Hizen-Kashima Station (~15 min)
description: One of Japan's three greatest Inari shrines, and the most architecturally dramatic in Kyushu. The main hall is built on stilts over a forested hillside using the same method as Kyoto's Kiyomizudera.
